CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and presents a logical flow from problem motivation through method and experiments; the main contributions are stated early and the methodology (two-stage approach) is easy to follow. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, while figures/tables and examples are described as effective and informative. The writing quality is good with thorough related work coverage, though the discussion of linguistic-rule coverage and limitations suggests a few places where greater clarity or more explicit examples could help readers reproduce the setup. From a presentation perspective it is strong with only minor clarifications needed. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early, with a clear two-stage method description and experiments on multiple Chinese and English datasets. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported; related work coverage is thorough and writing quality is described as good. Figures/tables and examples are reported as effective, though the paper notes limited linguistic rules which could have been illustrated more concretely. From a practitioner perspective the presentation is strong with only minor presentation issues to address.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and presents a logical flow from problem motivation through method and experiments; the main contributions are stated early and the methodology (two-stage approach) is easy to follow. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, while figures/tables and examples are described as effective and informative. The writing quality is good with thorough related work coverage, though the discussion of linguistic-rule coverage and limitations suggests a few places where greater clarity or more explicit examples could help readers reproduce the setup. From a presentation perspective it is strong with only minor clarifications needed.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early, with a clear two-stage method description and experiments on multiple Chinese and English datasets. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported; related work coverage is thorough and writing quality is described as good. Figures/tables and examples are reported as effective, though the paper notes limited linguistic rules which could have been illustrated more concretely. From a practitioner perspective the presentation is strong with only minor presentation issues to address.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper presents a reasonably clear two-stage integration of existing ideas—linear projection-based hypernym scoring, confidence-weighting, and transductive/label-propagation style non-linear optimization with soft linguistic constraints—which is a coherent but incremental combination rather than a radically new paradigm. The formulation is a well-studied problem (hypernym prediction), and while the authors differentiate their contribution from prior projection and rule-based work and show cross-lingual applicability, the technical ingredients themselves are familiar and do not introduce fundamentally new theory or mechanisms. An expert would find the engineering and empirical integration informative but not surprising. | B(3): The paper combines several existing ideas—linear projection models for hypernymy, transductive/label-propagation style optimization, and hand-crafted linguistic constraints—into a unified, confidence-weighted two-stage framework applied to Chinese hypernym prediction. While the specific integration (confidence-weighted initial scores + soft linguistic rules + non-linear transductive refinement) is practically useful and clearly differentiated from prior single-stage projection or rule-only methods, it is largely a non-trivial engineering synthesis rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. An experienced researcher would view this as a solid, pragmatic extension with moderate novelty rather than a breakthrough.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a reasonably clear two-stage integration of existing ideas—linear projection-based hypernym scoring, confidence-weighting, and transductive/label-propagation style non-linear optimization with soft linguistic constraints—which is a coherent but incremental combination rather than a radically new paradigm. The formulation is a well-studied problem (hypernym prediction), and while the authors differentiate their contribution from prior projection and rule-based work and show cross-lingual applicability, the technical ingredients themselves are familiar and do not introduce fundamentally new theory or mechanisms. An expert would find the engineering and empirical integration informative but not surprising.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper combines several existing ideas—linear projection models for hypernymy, transductive/label-propagation style optimization, and hand-crafted linguistic constraints—into a unified, confidence-weighted two-stage framework applied to Chinese hypernym prediction. While the specific integration (confidence-weighted initial scores + soft linguistic rules + non-linear transductive refinement) is practically useful and clearly differentiated from prior single-stage projection or rule-only methods, it is largely a non-trivial engineering synthesis rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. An experienced researcher would view this as a solid, pragmatic extension with moderate novelty rather than a breakthrough.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper tackles a core hypernym prediction problem with solid empirical evaluation across Chinese and English datasets and that the methodological contributions are practically relevant for semi-/transductive settings. I assign a 4 because, while the work is a good fit and shows consistent gains, remaining concerns about limited theoretical guarantees, language-specific focus, and lack of released code/reproducibility prevent a full top score.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP/ML topic—hypernym (is‑a) prediction—introducing a transductive non‑linear framework that integrates labeled/unlabeled data, linear initializers, and soft linguistic rules; the use of multiple Chinese datasets (FD, BK) and English benchmarks (BLESS, Shwartz) makes it relevant to the venue's audience. Methods and evaluation (precision/recall/F) follow community norms and related work is well covered, but the work leans empirical and language‑specific and appears to lack released code and formal guarantees, which raises minor concerns about reproducibility and theoretical contribution for a top‑tier conference. Overall, it is a good fit with some scope/treatment caveats that prevent a perfect score.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP problem (hypernym prediction) with a practically-minded, multilingual approach—combining labeled and large unlabeled Chinese data, confidence-weighted initial projections, and soft linguistic constraints—which matches top-tier NLP/ML interests in semi-/transductive methods and practical deployment. Experiments show consistent gains on two Chinese datasets (FD, a new Baidu-derived BK) and competitive results on English (BLESS, Shwartz), and the evaluation (precision/recall/F) and reported hyperparameter details meet community standards. The focus on engineering choices that improve robustness in low-resource/noisy settings and clear positioning against relevant baselines makes it a strong fit for a top conference.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper compares against a broad and appropriate set of baselines, evaluates on relevant Chinese and English benchmarks, reports metrics and hyperparameters, and provides component analyses. Reviewer A’s concerns about a few omitted canonical alternatives and the absence of released code are valid but minor relative to the overall thoroughness and fairness of the empirical comparisons, so a top score is warranted.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a reasonable set of prior methods (Fu et al., Wang & He, Li et al., Mirza & Tonelli, Lenci & Benotto, Santus et al.) on the same datasets (FD, BK, BLESS, Shwartz) using standard metrics, and explicitly acknowledges when prior methods are competitive (English results). Differences to baselines are described and hyperparameters are reported. However, as a theory-focused reviewer I note minor omissions of canonical alternatives (e.g., explicit label-propagation baselines or well‑tuned calibrated linear projection variants) and no mention of released code to fully verify parity, so the comparisons are good but not exhaustively comprehensive.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares to a broad set of established baselines (Fu et al., Wang & He, Li et al., Mirza & Tonelli, Lenci & Benotto, Santus et al.) and evaluates on both Chinese datasets (FD, BK) and English benchmarks (BLESS, Shwartz) using the same metrics (P/R/F), which matches realistic multilingual expectations I care about. The authors report hyperparameters, limitations, and explicitly note when their method is only competitive (not state-of-the-art) on English, and they explain how their approach differs (integration of transductive non-linear learning, soft linguistic rules, confidence-weighted initialization) with ablations/analysis of these components. Related work coverage is described as thorough, so comparisons appear comprehensive and fair for the claims made.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important, under-resourced problem and delivers a pragmatic, empirically effective transductive framework that yields consistent gains on Chinese datasets and competitive English results. I assign a 4 because the work is likely to influence practitioners by integrating unlabeled data and soft linguistic constraints in a usable way, while its impact is appropriately tempered by limited rule coverage, reliance on pretrained embeddings and hyperparameter tuning, and lack of stronger theoretical guarantees or clear code release.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper targets an important and under-resourced problem—Chinese hypernym prediction—and presents a practical transductive non-linear framework that empirically outperforms prior methods on two Chinese datasets (FD, BK) and is competitive on English benchmarks (BLESS, Shwartz). While this approach of combining confidence-weighted linear projections with soft linguistic constraints is useful and could be adopted by practitioners working on cross-lingual or resource-scarce taxonomy induction, its impact is tempered by limited rule coverage, dependence on pretrained embeddings and hyperparameter tuning, and no indication of released code or stronger theoretical guarantees. Overall the work is a solid, usable contribution that enables further applied and empirical follow-ups but is unlikely to fundamentally change theoretical perspectives in the area.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important, practical problem—Chinese hypernym prediction—by combining labeled and large unlabeled corpora, confidence-weighted linear projections, and soft linguistic rules in a transductive non-linear framework, showing consistent gains on two Chinese datasets (FD, BK) and competitive results on English (BLESS, Shwartz). From an applied-NLP perspective the approach is pragmatic and likely to influence practitioners: it integrates linguistic constraints and unlabeled data in a plug-inable way and reports hyperparameter details. Its impact is tempered by reliance on a few handcrafted rules and not being definitively state-of-the-art for English, but it nonetheless opens useful directions for multilingual and semi-supervised hypernym induction.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper clearly states its claims and is supported by empirical results across multiple datasets, with reasonable methodology and hyperparameter reporting. They also concur on weaknesses: limited ablation studies, missing statistical significance testing, and some omitted baselines/reproducibility details. Given the substantive experimental support and coherent formulation but notable gaps that prevent a higher rating, a score of 4 fairly balances strengths and remaining methodological concerns.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear claims and provides empirical evidence across Chinese (FD, BK) and English (BLESS, Shwartz) datasets, with hyperparameters reported and comparisons to several prior works, so results broadly support the main empirical claim. However, from the summary there are no formal guarantees or convergence proofs for the transductive non‑linear optimizer, and I see no mention of sufficient ablation studies (>=3) isolating key components such as confidence weighting, rule priors, and the non‑linear mapping—this weakens causal attribution. Experimental methodology is reasonable but appears to omit comparisons to some simple yet strong baselines (e.g., classic label propagation or calibrated linear projections) and code/reproducibility release is not mentioned, limiting verification. Overall the contribution is promising but has notable gaps in theoretical justification, ablations, and baseline breadth, so I rate it a 3.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and largely supported by experiments showing consistent gains on two Chinese datasets (FD, BK) and competitive results on English (BLESS, Shwartz), and the methodology (two-stage projection + transductive optimization with soft linguistic constraints) is well-motivated. Mathematical formulation appears coherent for a practical transductive objective, though no formal proofs are necessary and none are claimed; completeness of derivations cannot be fully judged from the summary but nothing indicates a technical error. Experimental methodology is reasonable with appropriate baselines and hyperparameter reporting, but the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or ablations that would strengthen claims about robustness to rule/confidence noise. Some assumptions (quality of unlabeled Baike data, limited handcrafted rules, and reliability of confidence estimates) are acknowledged but remain potential limitations, so conclusions are justified overall with minor caveats.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper provides a solid empirical evaluation across Chinese and English datasets, reports hyperparameters, and discusses limitations and related work. The main shortcoming cited by both is insufficient ablation/sensitivity analyses and unclear code/data release, which prevents a top score but does not undermine the empirical contributions; therefore a score of 4 reflects strong substance with some important reproducibility/analysis gaps to address.

reviewer_a.justification: The experimental evaluation is adequate — multiple Chinese datasets (FD, BK) and two English sets (BLESS, Shwartz) with reasonable baselines are used and hyperparameters are reported — supporting the main empirical claims. However, I see important gaps: the paper does not report code availability and lacks mention of thorough ablation/sensitivity studies (my persona requires ≥3 isolating ablations) and does not compare to some simple but strong baselines (e.g., label propagation or calibrated linear projections). Related work coverage appears thorough and the authors discuss several limitations, but reproducibility and deeper analyses of component contributions are insufficient for a higher score.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents solid empirical evaluation: it demonstrates consistent gains on two Chinese datasets (FD, BK) and shows transfer to English (BLESS, Shwartz), and reports hyperparameter choices and regularization details. The authors discuss limitations (few handcrafted rules, dependency on embeddings) and compare extensively to prior work, which aligns with my pragmatic priorities for multilingual and practical methods. However, the summary does not clearly indicate dedicated ablation/sensitivity studies (e.g., effect of individual linguistic rules or confidence weighting) nor whether code/data are released for full reproducibility, so there are minor gaps preventing a top score.

main_claims: Proposes a transductive non-linear learning framework that integrates labeled data, unlabeled data, initial linear projection models and linguistic rules for Chinese hypernym (is-a) prediction.
The method outperforms recent state-of-the-art approaches for Chinese hypernym prediction on two Chinese datasets (FD and BK).
The framework is language-general enough to be applied to English datasets, achieving competitive performance on BLESS and Shwartz datasets.
The model can incorporate arbitrary soft linguistic constraints and confidence-weighted initial predictions to improve robustness.
_summary.methodology: A two-stage approach: (1) train initial linear projection models on positive and negative labeled pairs to produce scores and confidences for unlabeled pairs; (2) perform transductive non-linear learning that jointly optimizes final prediction scores F by minimizing weighted deviation from initial predictions and soft linguistic-rule constraints, using nonlinear mappings and label propagation-style regularization.
datasets: Chinese corpus from Baidu Baike (1.2M entity pages, ~1.1B words)
limitations: Only a few handcrafted linguistic rules are used; linguistic rules cover only a few circumstances.
The method is not necessarily state-of-the-art for English (authors report competitive but not top results).
Dependent on pretrained embeddings and hyperparameter tuning (θ, regularization) as reported; linguistic rules and confidence estimates may be imperfect.

reviewer_a.name: Theory Purist
reviewer_a.background: Senior researcher in machine learning with a focus on theoretical guarantees for structured prediction and semi-/transductive learning; PhD in CS and experience reviewing for NeurIPS and ICLR.
traits: Penalises papers that present empirically-driven methods without formal justification or provable properties (e.g., no convergence proof, no bounds) — likely to subtract points if absent.
Demands comprehensive ablation studies and sensitivity analyses (hyperparameters, regularization, confidence weighting); deducts score if fewer than 3 ablations isolating key components are shown.

Requires comparisons to strong and diverse baselines including simpler well-tuned alternatives (e.g., label propagation, calibrated linear projections); penalises if top baselines or canonical methods are missing.
Strict about reproducibility: will lower score if code, hyperparameters, random seeds, or dataset splits are not fully released or described.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Prioritises principled understanding and reproducibility — rewards theoretical insight and clear ablations, and is harsh on purely empirical gains without explanation.

reviewer_b.name: Applied NLP Pragmatist
reviewer_b.background: Experienced NLP practitioner and senior conference reviewer with a track record of deploying relation extraction and taxonomy induction systems in multilingual settings; backgrounds include CoNLL and ACL reviewing.
traits: Rewards strong empirical improvements on realistic datasets and multilingual applicability; awards points for consistent gains across multiple datasets (especially language-specific like Chinese) and transfer to English.

Favors methods that integrate linguistic knowledge and unlabeled data (semi-/transductive approaches); will give credit for practical design choices that improve robustness in low-resource or noisy settings.
Lenient about lack of formal proofs if the method is well-motivated and shows reproducible empirical benefits; tolerates moderate ablations if key comparisons and intuitive analyses are present.

Values clarity about how to incorporate external constraints and confidence weighting in practical pipelines; gives positive credit if described in implementation-level detail or demonstrated to improve robustness.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Pragmatic evaluator who prioritises empirical impact, multilingual realism, and methods that can be plugged into practical pipelines; willing to accept fewer theoretical guarantees when experiments are thorough and convincing.
